coredata.dc:title: Application of graphene quantum dots in the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− and analysis of detection mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Yuan, Zihan
Wang, Mingkun
Zhu, Lin
Cheng, Xiao
Cao, Duxia
Guan, Ruifang
Zhou, Chuanjian
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of carbon material that have received widespread attention in recent years. However, due to their complex structure, it is difficult to explore their fluorescence emission mechanism and application mechanism. In this work, GQDs with simple structure were prepared using citric acid as a single precursor. The fluorescence properties and structure of GQDs were characterized, and the results confirmed that their luminescence originated from the n → π* electronic transition produced by the oxygen-containing functional groups in the edge state. Significantly, GQDs can be used as excellent probes to detect Hg2+ and ClO− in aqueous solution. Hg2+ combines with the carboxyl groups of the edge states of GQDs to form a complex, and then reduces the fluorescence emission through non-radiative electron transfer. ClO− can oxidize the hydroxyl groups in the edge states of GQDs to make them positively charged, which prevents electron transfer related to the luminescent center and reduces fluorescence emission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ion detection
Fluorescence mechanism
Citric acid
Electron transfer
Edge state

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and ClO−
Fluorescence properties and luminescence mechanism of GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image (a), AFM image (b), FT-IR spectrum (c), XPS full spectrum (d), C1s high-resolution spectrum (e) and O1s high-resolution spectra (f) of GQDs.
Structure model of GQDs and response mechanism to Hg2+ and ClO−.

(a) Absorption, optimal excitation and optimal emission spectra of GQDs. (b) The emission spectra of GQDs under different excitation wavelengths (280 nm–420 nm). (c) Changes in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs under different pH (λex = 366 nm).

(a) The blue bar is the fluorescence intensity of GQDs after adding 100.0 μM different metal ions, and the red bar is the fluorescence intensity after adding Hg2+ at the presence of other metal ions (100.0 μM). (b) The fluorescence response of GQDs after adding Hg2+ of different concentrations (0–65.0 μM), λex = 366 nm. Inset in (b) shows relationship between the concentration of Hg2+ (0–65.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity (464 nm) of GQDs. (c) Correlation between the concentration of Hg2+ (0–65.0 μM) and fluorescence intensity of GQDs. (d) Linear relationship between the concentration of Hg2+ (2.5–45.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity of GQDs.

(a) The black bar is the fluorescence response of GQDs after adding 10.0 μM different ions, and the red bar is the fluorescence response after the addition of ClO− at the presence of other ions (10.0 μM). (b) The fluorescence response of GQDs after adding ClO− of different concentrations (0–8.0 μM), λex = 366 nm. Inset in (b) shows the relationship between the concentration of ClO− (0–8.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity (464 nm) of GQDs. (c) Correlation between the concentration of ClO− (0–8.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. (d) Linear relationship between the concentration of ClO− (0.25–5.0 μM) and fluorescence intensity of GQDs.

(a) FT-IR spectra of GQDs, GQDs+Hg2+ system and GQDs+ClO− system. (b) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of Hg2+, GQDs and GQDs+Hg2+ system, and the optimal excitation and emission spectra of GQDs and GQDs+Hg2+ system. (c) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of ClO−, GQDs and GQDs+ClO− system, and the optimal excitation and emission spectra of GQDs and GQDs+ClO− system. (d) Fluorescence spectra after the addition of biothiols (Cys, Hcy, GSH) to the GQDs+Hg2+ system. (e) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs, GQDs+ClO− system, GQDs+ClO− + NaBH4 system and GQDs+NaBH4 system.

(a) and (b) are the C1s and O1s spectra of GQDs, respectively. (c) and (d) are the C1s and O1s spectra of GQDs+Hg2+, respectively. (e) and (f) are C1s spectrum and O1s spectrum of GQDs+ClO−, respectively.

head.ce:title: Application of graphene quantum dots in the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− and analysis of detection mechanism

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of carbon material that have received widespread attention in recent years. However, due to their complex structure, it is difficult to explore their fluorescence emission mechanism and application mechanism. In this work, GQDs with simple structure were prepared using citric acid as a single precursor. The fluorescence properties and structure of GQDs were characterized, and the results confirmed that their luminescence originated from the n → π* electronic transition produced by the oxygen-containing functional groups in the edge state. Significantly, GQDs can be used as excellent probes to detect Hg2+ and ClO− in aqueous solution. Hg2+ combines with the carboxyl groups of the edge states of GQDs to form a complex, and then reduces the fluorescence emission through non-radiative electron transfer. ClO− can oxidize the hydroxyl groups in the edge states of GQDs to make them positively charged, which prevents electron transfer related to the luminescent center and reduces fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:para: As the source of life, water is extremely important to human life and development, and the safety of water sources is the top priority. As one of the heavy metal ions, Hg2+ has an accumulation effect in humans, animals and plants. If people drink water contaminated by Hg2+ for a long time, the human body will be poisoned, and neurasthenia syndrome, memory loss and many other adverse reactions will occur [1–4]. Therefore, it is of great significance to quickly and effectively detect Hg2+ in water sources. In addition, since water sources contain a large number of pathogenic microorganisms, the drinking water in daily life must be disinfected. Hypochlorite has been widely used in tap water disinfection due to its highly effective antibacterial effect, but excessive hypochlorite can also cause some diseases, such as cardiovascular disease, kidney disease, arthritis and cancer [5–8]. Therefore, it is necessary to detect the content of hypochlorite ion (ClO−) in the water phase through physical or chemical methods. A variety of methods have been developed to detect Hg2+ and ClO− in the environment, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), and atomic absorption/fluorescence spectroscopy [9–12]. However, these test methods require expensive instruments, cumbersome and time-consuming operation processes, and highly specialized knowledge, which limits their wide applications. In comparison, fluorescence spectroscopy is widely used in the field of ion detection due to its high sensitivity, simple operation steps, good selectivity and low price [13–15]. However, most fluorescent probe materials can only detect one type of ion. In addition, due to the diversity of synthetic raw materials and the complexity of some probes' structures, it is difficult to analyze the detection mechanism. Therefore, it is a promising research direction to synthesize fluorescent materials with simple structure and capable of responding to Hg2+ and ClO− simultaneously in the water phase.

GQDs have become current research hotspots due to their unique structure, excellent light stability, good biocompatibility and water solubility, and advanced applications in the fields of biological sciences and environmental sciences [16–20]. At present, more and more “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods have been developed to prepare GQDs [16,18–27]. Among them, the “bottom-up” method has been widely studied because of its simple operation, wide range of raw materials and the ability to precisely control the shape and distribution of the product [18–20,28]. In order to improve certain properties of GQDs (for example: higher luminous efficiency, different emission wavelengths, longer fluorescence lifetime, etc.), researchers often use two or more materials as precursors to prepare various doped GQDs, which, to a certain extent, brings difficulties to understand the luminescence mechanism of GQDs.

Therefore, in this work, a one-step pyrolysis method was used to synthesize simple-structured GQDs with only citric acid as the precursor. The synthesized GQDs can respond to Hg2+ and ClO− simultaneously, and the response is sensitive and fast, and the detection limit is low. The luminescence mechanism and ion detection mechanism were analyzed in detail through the characterization of its fluorescence properties and structure.
Citric acid, sodium hydroxide and inorganic salts were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

Preparation of GQDs was done on the basis of the modification of previous method [16,29]. 3.0 g anhydrous citric acid was added to a round-bottomed flask, and heated in an oil bath heater at 200 °C. It can be observed that the citric acid melted after heating for about 5 min. The crude orange graphene quantum dot products were obtained by keeping heating for 30 min. Under continuous vigorous stirring, the resulting crude graphene product was added dropwise to the pre-prepared NaOH solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL, and the mixture was kept stirring for 30 min after the dropwise addition, and then the pH of the final product was adjusted to 7.0. The moisture in the product was evaporated by freeze-drying, and finally an orange-yellow solid sample was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence tests were performed at room temperature, and the excitation wavelength was set to 366 nm. During the test, a GQDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL was first prepared. Then 2 mL of the above solution was poured into a cuvette, and a certain amount of ionic solution was added. Finally, the solution was mixed for 2 min to make it completely mixed, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded.

The functional groups of GQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (a NICOLET-iS50 spectrometer). The morphology of GQDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscope. The absorption value of GQDs was measured with an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer). A fluorescence spectrometer (a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer) was used to determine the optimal excitation wavelength, optimal emission wavelength, fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs. The elemental composition of GQDs was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS; ESCALAB 250XI). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the GQDs were obtained via a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (FEI Tecnai F20).

GQDs were prepared by one-step pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid followed by dehydration and carbonization. The structure of GQDs was characterized by HRTEM, AFM, FT-IR and XPS respectively. As shown in Fig. 1a, GQDs have good dispersibility, with a size distribution of 15–25 nm, and the center of the prepared product shows high crystallinity, with obvious lattice stripes. The lattice spacing is 0.23 nm, which corresponds to the (1120) crystal face [22,30], indicating that the anhydrous citric acid produces a graphite structure after carbonization. The results of atomic force microscopy show that the height of GQDs is mainly distributed in the range below 2.0 nm, suggesting that there is a multilayer graphene oxide-like structures (Fig. 1b) [30]. The results of AFM (Fig. 1b) and HRTEM (Fig. 1a) both indicate that GQDs have been successfully prepared.

As shown in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1c), the peaks at 2950 and 825 cm−1 can be assigned to the CH stretching mode and CH out-of-plane bending mode. The peaks at 1600 and 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO−, respectively, indicating that the structure of GQDs contains –COOH groups. In addition, a characteristic absorption band with C–OH extending at 1272 cm−1 is also observed. The broad absorption peak near 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the broad characteristic peak of OH. The above results indicate that the carbonization of citric acid was not complete, and there were a large number of organic functional groups in the structure. From the XPS full spectrum in Fig. 1d, it can be seen that the obtained product contains three elements: C, O, and Na, and their relative contents are 42.07%, 38.35%, and 19.58%, respectively. This indicates that the structure of the product is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, where the Na element comes from the sodium hydroxide aqueous solution introduced during adjusting the pH.

The type of functional group of GQDs was further confirmed by XPS high-resolution spectra and the results are shown in Fig. 1e and f. The high-resolution C1s spectra of XPS show four peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.39 eV, 288.23 eV and 289.35 eV, corresponding to C=C/C–C/C–H, CO, CO and O=C–O, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum is divided into three peaks, which are located at 530.09 eV, 532.02 eV and 535.72 eV, corresponding to CO, O=C–O and O–H/bound water respectively. From the high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s, we can conclude that the as-prepared GQDs have a graphene structure and are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, etc. (Fig. 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and luminescence mechanism of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The product GQDs is a light yellow transparent solution under natural light. Under UV light (365 nm), GQDs show blue luminescence. The UV absorption spectrum in Fig. 3a shows broad absorption peaks centered at 252 nm and 354 nm, which are derived from the π → π* electronic transition (sp2 carbon core state, CC) and n → π* electronic transition (edge state, hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl), respectively [20]. The optimal excitation wavelength is 366 nm, showing a very large overlap with the absorption peak at 354 nm, which means that the fluorescence emission may be related to the n → π* electronic transition. In addition, its maximum emission wavelength is 464 nm, which is consistent with the blue light emission under ultraviolet light, and the fluorescence quantum yield is 4.54%. As shown in Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelength is changed between 280 and 420 nm, no shift in emission wavelength is observed, showing the characteristics of no excitation wavelength dependence and similar to non-aromatic fluorescent materials [5,31,32]. In non-aromatic luminescent materials, clusters containing lone pairs of electrons (carbonyl, amino, mercapto, cyano, hydroxyl, amide, etc.) form a space electron delocalization system, which will further produce fluorescence emission, confirming that the luminescence of GQDs originates from n → π* electronic transition. Citric acid was used as a single precursor to synthesize GQDs, and the functional groups containing lone pairs of electrons were only hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl related to the oxygen atom. While the GQDs were surface passivated during the synthesis process, and hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups only existed at the edges of the structure [17], so fluorescence emission are originated from the edge state of GQDs.

Then the influence of solvent pH on the fluorescence performance of GQDs was studied. From Fig. 3c, it can be seen that when the pH value is between 6.2 and 13.0, the fluorescence intensity is relatively stable, and when the pH value is below 6, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly. This phenomenon can be explained as the following: At low pH, the increase of H+ concentration causes the protonation of GQDs, and the empty orbital provided by the hydrogen nucleus is easy to combine with the lone electron pair on O, which binds the n → π* electron transition and reduces the fluorescence intensity. When the pH value increases, the hydroxide ion has a small effect on the electronic structure of O, so the fluorescence intensity is not significantly reduced. It may also be that the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process in the GQDs receptor (fluorophore) is thermodynamically disallowed under acidic conditions. While under alkaline conditions, the deprotonation is beneficial to the receptor, resulting in a PET phenomenon and strong fluorescence emission [33]. This means that GQDs can act as ON-OFF sensor between pH 6.2 and 4.0. In short, the pH value of 7.0 is selected as the unified standard in the following research.

As shown in Figs. S1a and S2a, the rate of response of GQDs to ions was first studied. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreases after adding Hg2+ and ClO−, and the response reaches a stable state after 2 min. Therefore, 2 min after adding ions is selected as the optimal response time. Moreover, the response time shows that the quenching effect of ClO− and Hg2+ on GQDs is fast and stable, which means that the time limit in practical applications is reduced, showing a good application prospect.

Then the optimal response pH was studied. As shown in Fig. S1b, the difference between the maximum intensity (464 nm) of the GQDs solution with and without Hg2+ is the largest and the response is relatively stable in the range of pH 6.2 to 9.1. At low pH (pH < 6.2), the protonation of GQDs leads to a significant decrease in fluorescence, which is not conducive to the ion detection process. Under alkaline conditions (pH > 9.1), Hg2+ reacts with OH− to form Hg(OH)2, and Hg(OH)2 can be easily converted into HgO precipitates, therefore Hg2+ cannot be effectively detected. Similarly, it is found that there are optimal detection conditions for ClO− detection in the range of pH 6.2 to 10.3 (Fig. S2b). The weakening of the detection effect under alkaline conditions may be due to the reduction of the ability of ClO−. Therefore, the pH value of 7.0 is selected as the unified standard in the following research.

Fig. S3 provides the images of GQDs solution under UV lamp in the presence of different ions. It can be clearly seen that only Hg2+ and ClO− can completely quench the fluorescence of GQDs. After optimizing the experimental conditions, a more detailed selectivity and competition test for common metal cations and anions was performed, as shown in Fig. 4a and Fig. 5a.

In the Hg2+ detection experiment, in addition to Hg2+, 13 other metal ions response effects were also explored, including Cu2+, Fe2+, Sn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Na+, Mg2+, Cd2+, K+. As the blue bar shown in Fig. 4a, we can see that these metal ions have almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs, which proves that GQDs can specifically detect Hg2+. In addition, in order to avoid the interference from other ions, a competitive reaction detection experiment was carried out. And the result is shown in the red bar in Fig. 4a, revealing that the presence of other ions has little effect on quenching Hg2+.

In the ClO− detection experiment, 4 common anions (I−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, SCN−) and H2O2 were introduced. From the black bar in Fig. 5a, it can be seen that these ions hardly change the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. More importantly, this indicates that the presence of other ions has no effect on the detection of ClO− (red bar in Fig. 5a).

The sensitivity detection of Hg2+ and ClO− was continued to be explored, and I
0
-I/I
0 is as the ordinate (I
0 is the maximum fluorescence intensity of the blank sample, I is the maximum fluorescence intensity after adding the response ion). First, the sensitivity response to Hg2+ was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 4b. With the increase of the concentration of Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreases rapidly. After linear fitting (Fig. 4c), it is found that the concentration of Hg2+ between 2.5 and 45.0 μM has a linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching efficiency of GQDs (Fig. 4d), and the regression equation is I
0
-I/I
0 = 0.0238 + 0.0208 C 1, and C 1 represents the concentration of Hg2+. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) is 0.23 μM, which is lower than that of most previously reported Hg2+ detection assays (Table S1). Similarly, as shown in Fig. 5b and c, as the concentration of ClO− increases, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreases rapidly, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of GQDs shows a linear relationship with the concentration of ClO− in the range of 0.25–5.0 μM (Fig. 5d), which satisfies the linear equation I
0
-I/I
0 = 0.0065 + 0.1757 C
2 , C
2 is the concentration of ClO−. The calculated LOD is 22.1 nM, which is much lower than that of some other polymer dots, quantum dots, and organic conjugated fluorescent materials (Table S2). Such a wide response range and low detection limit prove that GQDs have the potential to be used in the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− in the environment.

In the previous literature, a series of fluorescent materials were used to respond to anions and cations in various environments, and ion detection mechanisms were also diverse (Table S3). It has been confirmed that the luminescence of GQDs comes from the oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl) on the surface, and the clear and simple structure of GQDs is conducive to the analysis of the ion detection mechanism. By comparing the changes of FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra before and after the addition of ions, as well as the difference in UV absorption, a conjecture about the mechanism of ion detection was proposed. From the FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 6a, it can be observed that after adding Hg2+ to the GQDs, the OH stretching vibration peak intensity weakens and becomes sharp, and the symmetric stretching vibration peak corresponding to COO− at 1400 cm−1 is also significantly weakened. In addition, as shown in Fig. 7a–d that, compared with GQDs, the binding energy of OH does not change significantly after adding Hg2+, but the binding energy of O=C–O has a greater change. Therefore, we speculate that the changes in FT-IR and XPS spectra are related to carboxylic acid groups. It is worth noting that Fig. 6b shows that Hg2+ does not produce absorption by itself, but after adding Hg2+ to the GQDs solution, the absorption peak at 354 nm disappears and a new shoulder peak is formed at around 315 nm. Therefore, we believe that Hg2+ is complexed with the carboxyl group on the edge of GQDs to form a complex, and then the fluorescence emission is reduced by non-radiative electron transfer between GQDs and Hg2+ (Fig. 2) [34–38]. Next, the changes of NMR and TEM of GQDs before and after binding Hg2+ were studied (Figs. S4 and S5). In the 1H NMR spectrum, all the proton peaks of GQDs are basically unchanged after binding Hg2+, which should be that the proton peak signals of the carboxyl group bound to Hg2+ cannot be observed when D2O is used as the solvent. In the 13C NMR spectrum, it can be observed that all the carbon signals of the GQDs slightly move to the high field after binding Hg2+, and the signals assigned to carboxyl carbon (COOH) near 180 ppm have a more obvious chemical shift compared with other carbon signals. Therefore, this also proves that Hg2+ only complexes with the carboxyl group of the edge state of GQDs, and basically does not change the structure of its core state. The TEM image of the GQDs+Hg2+ system shows that the lattice fringe spacing and morphology of GQDs basically did not change before and after combination with Hg2+. This is because Hg2+ only complexes with the carboxyl groups of the edge states of GQDs, and does not affect the overall structure of GQDs, which is consistent with the NMR results. In addition, the optimal excitation wavelength and optimal emission wavelength of the GQDs+Hg2+ system are the same as GQDs, which confirms that the emission center of GQDs has not change and no new fluorescent substances have been produced. Interestingly, after GQDs are quenched by Hg2+, the addition of biothiol can restore the fluorescence of GQDs (Fig. 6d). The reason is that the binding force between biothiol and Hg2+ is stronger, and the fluorescence can be restored by peeling off the Hg2+ on the surface of GQDs, which also proves that the method has the potential to be applied to the detection of biological thiols.

According to literature reports [5], the hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots or quantum dots have a great contribution to luminescence. ClO− is a very strong oxidant, which can reduce the fluorescence emission by oxidizing the hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots and changing its surface state [6,39]. From the FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 7a, it can be observed that the intensity of OH stretching vibration peak is significantly reduced, which is consistent with the literature report, indicating that ClO− also interacts with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 7e and f, compared with GQDs, after adding ClO−, the proportion of all functional groups and the position of binding energy do not change significantly, which may be due to the reduction of content of the hydroxyl on the surface of GQDs. This confirms that trace hydroxyl groups contribute greatly to the luminescence of GQDs, and this is also the reason for the extremely low detection limit of ClO−. In addition, Fig. 6c shows that GQDs and ClO− have very strong absorptions at 254 nm and 295 nm, respectively, while both the absorption peaks are disappeared after adding ClO− to the GQDs solution. Therefore, we believe that the addition of ClO− oxidizes the hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs and inhibits the electron transfer between the hydroxyl groups (from the n → π* electron transition in the edge state) and the luminescent center, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence (Fig. 2) [5]. Previous studies have shown that NaBH4 can selectively reduce the carbonyl and epoxy groups on the surface of GQDs to hydroxyl groups [40,41]. It is worth noting that after the GQDs are oxidized by ClO−, the addition of the reducing agent NaBH4 cannot restore the fluorescence effectively (Fig. 6e), which may be because the carbon atom connected to the hydroxyl group does not contain hydrogen atoms, and ClO− only oxidizes the hydroxyl group to the intermediate state GQDs·+ (reactions (1) and (2)), similar to the protonation process (Fig. 3c, reaction (3)). Fig. 6e also shows that adding NaBH4 to the GQDs solution does not affect the luminescence, which is consistent with previous report [16].
(1)
GQDs + Cl
O −
+
H 2
O → GQ
Ds
· +
+ · Cl + 2 O
H −
(2)
GQDs + · Cl → GQD
s
· +
+ C
l −
(3)
GQDs − OH +
H +
→ GQDs − O
H 2 +

In this paper, citric acid was used as a single precursor to prepare GQDs with simple structure through a one-step pyrolysis method. The characterization of its structure and fluorescence properties confirm that the fluorescence emission originates from the n → π* transition produced by the oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl) in the surface state. In addition, GQDs can be used as excellent fluorescent probes to quickly and sensitively detect Hg2+ and ClO− in aqueous solutions. Hg2+ quenches the fluorescence by complexing with the carboxyl group of GQDs to form a complex with a detection limit of 0.23 μM. ClO− oxidizes the hydroxyl group on the edge of GQDs to cause fluorescence quenching, with a detection limit as low as 22.1 nM. The synthesized GQDs with a simple structure and clear structure provide a sensitive and rapid response to Hg2+ and ClO− in aqueous media, showing a good application prospect.
Hao Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.
Zihan Yuan: Data curation, Visualization, Validation.
Mingkun Wang: Visualization, Validation.
Lin Zhu: Validation.
Xiao Cheng: Supervision.
Duxia Cao: Writing - review & editing.
Ruifang Guan: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
Chuanjian Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21477047, 51903139) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MEM006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108454.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for the selective detection of Hg2+ and glutathione
Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, C. Chai, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, J. Lv, W. Bian, M.M.F. Choi, Microwave synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for the selective detection of Hg2+ and glutathione, Optical Materials 99 (2020) 109559.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent carbon dots as temperature sensors and “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols
Dyes Pigments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, J. You, J. Wang, X. Dong, R. Guan, D. Cao, Highly luminescent carbon dots as temperature sensors and “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols, Dyes and Pigments 173 (2020) 107950.

J. Hazard. Mater.
